attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with her parent who is concerned about her eating habits. Over the past year, she has become increasingly preoccupied with 'clean eating' and has eliminated most food groups from her diet. She spends several hours each day researching nutrition and preparing meals. She acknowledges that her beliefs about food may be extreme but maintains that a restricted diet is necessary for optimal health. She has lost 8 kg but does not believe she is too thin. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 62/min, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. Her body mass index is 18.5 kg/m2. Mental state examination reveals an anxious young woman with restricted affect who engages well in conversation. Her thought form is logical and there are no perceptual disturbances.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes her beliefs about food?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a young woman with extreme beliefs about food and nutrition that have significantly impacted her life. The key features that help identify this as an overvalued idea include: the belief is potentially understandable in the current sociocultural context of 'clean eating' movements; she has some insight that her beliefs may be extreme; and the belief is strongly held but not entirely fixed, meaning it is potentially modifiable with discussion and evidence.

A delusion is a fixed, false belief that is firmly maintained despite clear evidence to the contrary and is often bizarre or inconsistent with cultural norms. This patient exhibits some insight and her beliefs are not completely rigid or detached from reality, ruling out a delusion.

An obsession is an intrusive, unwanted, and distressing thought that causes anxiety and is recognized as irrational by the individual. This patient’s beliefs about food are ego-syntonic (aligned with her values), meaning they are not perceived as intrusive or distressing, distinguishing them from an obsession.

A phobia is an irrational fear of specific objects or situations that the person acknowledges as excessive. This patient's restrictive eating is based on rigid health beliefs rather than fear, so this does not meet the definition of a phobia.

Compulsions are repetitive, ritualistic behaviors performed to reduce anxiety, typically in response to obsessions. While she spends excessive time researching and preparing food, these behaviors are goal-directed rather than aimed at alleviating distress, making compulsions an unlikely explanation.

Overvalued ideas fall on the spectrum between normal beliefs and delusions. They are more intense and preoccupying than typical beliefs but still retain some degree of flexibility. They are often seen in conditions such as anorexia nervosa, body dysmorphic disorder, and illness anxiety disorder.
learningPoints: Overvalued ideas are strongly held beliefs that are potentially understandable in the context of a person's cultural and social background.
Unlike delusions, overvalued ideas are potentially modifiable with evidence and discussion.
The intensity of belief in overvalued ideas lies between normal beliefs and delusions on the conviction spectrum.
references: Hildebrandt T, Tuschen‐Caffier B, Hartmann A, et al. The role of overvaluation of shape and weight in eating disorders: A cognitive perspective. Behav Res Ther. 2007;45(7):1521–1532. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.brat.2007.05.006

Treasure J, Schmidt U. The cognitive-interpersonal maintenance model of anorexia nervosa revisited: a summary of the evidence for cognitive, socio-emotional and interpersonal precipitants of anorexia nervosa. J Eat Disord. 2013;1:13. Available at: https://jeatdisord.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/2050-2974-1-13

Fairburn CG, Cooper Z, Shafran R. Cognitive behaviour therapy for eating disorders: A “transdiagnostic” theory and treatment. Behav Res Ther. 2003;41(5):509–528. Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0005796702001171
Benjamin JS, Kaplan SJ. Sadock Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry.

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
